1) Renal carcinoma  
   - Pro: Enlarged kidney with irregular parenchyma; possible mass lesion.  
   - Con: No discrete mass described; presence of staghorn calculus and multiple hypodense cavitations with rim enhancement is not typical; fat-density xanthomas absent in carcinoma; urine stasis and calculi more suggestive of infection rather than primary tumor.

   - Pro: Chronic infection with calyceal cavitation; calcifications; perirenal fat stranding possible; TB can cause urothelial thickening.  
   - Con: TB usually causes caseating necrosis, granulomas, and cortical scarring; staghorn calculi and xanthomas less characteristic; no mention of caseating necrosis or typical granulomas; acute presentation with fever and calculi less typical.  
   - Verdict: Possible but less consistent with staghorn calculus and xanthomas.

   - Pro: Classic triad of staghorn calculus, enlarged kidney with multiple hypodense cavities and rim enhancement (âbear paw signâ); fat-density xanthomas (lipid-laden macrophages) on CT; extensive perirenal inflammation and ureteral involvement; nonfunctioning kidney on delayed imaging; elevated inflammatory markers and fever.  
   - Con: None significant; findings almost pathognomonic.  
   - Verdict: Pass (strongly fits all imaging and clinical features).

   - Pro: Kidney enlargement possible.  
   - Con: No masses described; calculi and xanthomas not typical; lymphomas do not cause staghorn calculi or xanthoma formation; no evidence of lymphadenopathy; clinical presentation more infective.  
   - Verdict: Fail.  

6) Malakoplakia  
   - Pro: Can occur in renal pelvis and cause obstruction; usually associated with chronic E.Coli infection; may cause xanthomatous macrophages.  
   - Con: Usually forms localized lesions, not diffuse cavities with staghorn calculi and extensive perirenal inflammation; no classic calcifications or "bear paw sign".  
   - Verdict: Less likely.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Xanthogranulomatous pyelonephritis (most consistent with all key imaging and clinical findings).  
2) Renal tuberculosis (possible but less typical for full picture).  
3) Abscess (infection present but imaging less typical).  
4) Malakoplakia (less extensive and typical imaging findings).  
5) Renal carcinoma (no typical mass lesion).  
6) Lymphoma (no typical features).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 29-year-old female patient presented to the cardiology department with episodes of numbness in both her upper and lower extremities, which spontaneously resolved after 30 minutes. She also reported episodes of chest pain that she attributed to stress. Heart auscultation was normal. ECG showed diffuse ST-elevations.
 Imaging Findings: Echocardiography (ECG) demonstrated severe systolic heart failure, with a left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) of 30%. The exam showed basal hypokinesia and preserved apical contractility, along with irregularities in the lining of the left ventricular myocardium (Figure 1).
 
 A contrast-enhanced computed tomography (CECT) scan revealed no coronary abnormalities but confirmed the irregularities in the left ventricle wall (Figure 2).
 
 Subsequently, a cardiac MRI was performed, which revealed moderate left ventricle dilation and irregularities in the ventricle wall, including protrusions of myocardial bridges, multiple crypts, and thinning of the inferolateral apex with diffuse dyskinesia (Figures 3 and 4). Severe left ventricular dysfunction was confirmed, with an LVEF of 20% and a global longitudinal strain (GLS) of -7.85%, as well as ventricular dyssynchrony. A non-ischemic fibrosis region was also present in the interventricular septum and the posterior hinge point. The right ventricle appeared to be normal in morphology and function.

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
1. Patient presents with transient neurological symptoms (numbness in extremities) and episodes of chest pain.  
2. ECG shows diffuse ST-elevations.  
3. Echocardiography shows severe systolic heart failure (LVEF 30%), basal hypokinesia with preserved apical contractility, and irregularities in the left ventricular myocardium.  
4. CECT shows no coronary artery abnormalities, confirms irregularities in the LV wall.  
5. Cardiac MRI shows moderate LV dilation, irregular LV wall with myocardial bridge protrusions, multiple crypts, thinning of the inferolateral apex, diffuse dyskinesia, severe LV dysfunction (LVEF 20%, GLS -7.85%), ventricular dyssynchrony.  
6. Non-ischemic fibrosis in interventricular septum and posterior hinge point.  
7. Right ventricle is normal.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1. Left ventricular noncompaction (LVNC):  
  • Pros: Irregularities in the LV wall, multiple crypts, ventricular dysfunction, non-compaction features.  
  • Cons: Typically shows prominent trabeculations predominantly in the apex and mid-ventricle, not just basal hypokinesia; also usually associated with prominent trabecular meshwork and deep intertrabecular recesses communicated with the LV cavity. No explicit mention of classic NC/C ratio or diagnostic criteria for LVNC directly in MRI findings.  
2. Saw-tooth cardiomyopathy:  
  • Pros: Rare, characterized by irregular LV endocardial surface and myocardial abnormalities, might present with protrusions and fibrosis; severe LV dysfunction and symptoms fit.  
  • Cons: Few case reports; the diagnosis requires characteristic saw-tooth appearance of the LV wall, which matches irregularities and protrusions but crypts and bridging are also noted; no standard diagnostic criteria but fits better than other options.  
3. Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM):  
  • Pros: Can have fibrosis and apical involvement.  
  • Cons: Typically hypertrophy is predominant, not mentioned here; basal hypokinesia with preserved apical contractility does not fit classic HCM; no mention of hypertrophy or LV outflow tract obstruction; fibrotic changes are present but not typical distribution; normal RV.  
4. Arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy (ACM):  
  • Pros: Right ventricle normal, but left ventriculopathy can occur; fibrosis and dyskinesia present.  
  • Cons: Most ACM involves RV primarily; no fibro-fatty infiltration mentioned; no RV wall abnormalities; fibrosis pattern not typical; no mention of fibro-fatty infiltration.  
C) Ranking:  
1. Saw-tooth cardiomyopathy – best matches irregular LV wall appearance, multiple crypts, protruding myocardial bridges, fibrosis, and severe LV dysfunction without coronary disease or RV involvement.  
2. Left ventricular noncompaction – fits some features (crypts, LV dysfunction), but lacks classic NC/C findings and hypertrophied trabeculations.  
3. Arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy – less likely due to normal RV and lack of fibro-fatty infiltration.  
4. Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy – least likely without hypertrophy or LVOT obstruction.  
</analysis>  
<final>  
Saw-tooth cardiomyopathy  
</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 6-month-old infant presented with a history of on-and-off soft tissue swelling over the left cheek since 2 months (Figure 1). No history of trauma. Full-term normal delivery.
 Imaging Findings: Head and neck CT with bone reconstruction showed cortical irregularity with sclerosis, solid periosteal reaction, increase in cortical width and density, seen along the angle and ramus of the left hemimandible (Figures 2a and 2b).
 
 On MRI, the corresponding area showed mild STIR hyperintensity (Figure 3) and T1 hypointensity (Figure 4). No evidence of any significant diffusion restriction was seen in this region (Figures 5a and 5b). Surrounding oedematous changes were seen in the left masticator muscles and infratemporal fossa. No focal discrete collection or solid component was seen.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 6-month-old infant presented with a history of on-and-off soft tissue swelling over the left cheek since 2 months. No history of trauma. Full-term normal delivery.

On MRI, the corresponding area showed mild STIR hyperintensity and T1 hypointensity. No evidence of any significant diffusion restriction was seen in this region. Surrounding oedematous changes were seen in the left masticator muscles and infratemporal fossa. No focal discrete collection or solid component was seen.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Osteomyelitis, Battered baby syndrome, Hypervitaminosis A, Hyperphosphataemia, Ewing sarcoma, Scurvy, Infantile cortical hyperostosis (Caffey disease), Malignant infantile hyperpetrosis, Prolonged PGE1 infusion

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Age: 6-month-old infant  
- On-and-off soft tissue swelling over the left cheek  
- Imaging (CT with bone reconstruction): cortical irregularity with sclerosis, solid periosteal reaction, increased cortical width and density along the angle and ramus of the left hemimandible  
- MRI: mild STIR hyperintensity and T1 hypointensity in the corresponding area, no significant diffusion restriction  
- Surrounding edema in left masticator muscles and infratemporal fossa  
- No discrete collection or solid tumor mass  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Osteomyelitis  
   - Pros: Periosteal reaction, marrow signal changes, surrounding edema are all compatible with osteomyelitis.  
   - Cons: No discrete abscess or soft tissue collection is identified; no diffusion restriction typically seen in acute infection.  
2) Battered baby syndrome  
   - Pros: Possible cause of facial swelling and bone changes.  
   - Cons: No history of trauma; distribution is localized to left mandible without multiple or fracture patterns typical in abuse; solid periosteal reaction and cortical changes are not typical solely from trauma without multiple injuries.  
3) Hypervitamin A  
   - Pros: Can cause periosteal reaction.  
   - Cons: Usually diffuse and affects multiple bones. No clinical correlation given; imaging localized to one mandible only, making this unlikely.  
4) Hyperphosphataemia  
   - Pros: Can cause bone changes and periosteal reaction.  
   - Cons: Rare; typically involves multiple sites and systemic manifestations not described here.  
5) Ewing sarcoma  
   - Pros: Aggressive cortico-medullary lesion with periosteal reaction possible.  
   - Cons: No soft tissue mass or diffusion restriction; mild marrow edema but no definitive aggressive tumor signs; no systemic symptoms described.  
6) Scurvy  
   - Pros: Periosteal reaction, cortical irregularities possible.  
   - Cons: Typically bilateral and involves metaphyses rather than mandible exclusively; clinical signs absent.  
7) Infantile cortical hyperostosis (Caffey disease)  
   - Pros: Classic age (infancy), unilateral mandibular involvement with cortical thickening, periosteal reaction, soft tissue swelling without abscess, normal diffusion, no tumor.  
   - Cons: None significant; imaging and clinical correlate well.  
8) Malignant infantile hyperpetrosis  
   - Pros: Similar to Caffey disease but malignant version.  
   - Cons: No aggressive lesion features or masses; no diffusion restriction; mild edema only.  
9) Prolonged PGE1 infusion  
   - Pros: Can cause new bone formation.  
   - Cons: No history of prolonged PGE1 infusion; unlikely cause here.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Infantile cortical hyperostosis (Caffey disease) - Pathognomonic for infant age, unilateral mandibular periosteal reaction with cortical thickening, soft tissue swelling without abscess or tumor. The imaging pattern and clinical age match exactly.  
2) Osteomyelitis - Possible but less likely due to lack of abscess or diffusion restriction, and clinical scenario seems less acute infection.  
3) Ewing sarcoma - Less likely; no aggressive tumor mass or diffusion.  
4) Scurvy - Less likely due to unilateral involvement and lack of typical systemic signs.  
5) Hypervitamin A, Hyperphosphataemia, Battered baby syndrome, Prolonged PGE1 infusion - Unlikely based on distribution, lack of trauma, and clinical context.  
6) Malignant infantile hyperpetrosis - Less likely due to lack of aggressive features.  

Therefore, the best diagnosis is Infantile cortical hyperostosis (Caffey disease).  
</analysis>  
<final>  
Infantile cortical hyperostosis (Caffey disease)  
</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 2-year-old male child was brought by his 34-year-old mother with complaints of abdominal pain and recurrent vomiting. The child was clinically stable, and the abdominal ultrasound was normal. However, we incidentally discovered that both the mother and the son had physical impairments in their bilateral upper and lower limbs.
 Imaging Findings: The child had a classical median cleft with rudimentary bilateral thumbs with loss of opposition movement (Figure 1). A frontal radiograph revealed a bilateral median cleft, and the right hand’s proximal phalanges of the middle and index fingers fused, resulting in syndactyly. An additional middle phalanx was observed in the right thumb. Bilaterally, the thumb and index finger were displaced toward each other (Figures 2a and 2b). The radiography of the child’s feet depicted the absence of three of his metatarsals (Figure 2c). The X-ray of the infant’s skull showed multiple convolutions, suggesting a copper-beaten appearance (Figure 3).
 
 Interestingly, his 34-year-old mother, too, suffered from a similar deformity. On clinical inspection, both her right and left hands showed the presence of only three fingers (Figure 4a). A hypoplastic index finger was seen in the left hand. Bilateral feet showed only two toes with possible syndactyly (Figure 4b).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 33-year-old female presents with a 6-year history of abnormal involuntary movements of all limbs, face, and lips, which initially started as increased fidgeting and finger flicking. Cognitive decline began a year later, with memory loss, disorientation, and difficulty dressing and eating, and now is fully dependent for activities of daily living.
 Imaging Findings: A contrast-enhanced MRI (CEMRI) of the brain was performed with 1.5T with sequences of T1WI inversion recovery 3D, T2WI axial and coronal, 3D FLAIR, SWI, DWI, and MR spectroscopy (MRS). MRI revealed atrophy of the head of the caudate bilaterally (Figures 1 and 2) with decreased FH:CC ratio of 1.18 (frontal horn width to intercaudate distance ratio) (Figure 5) and increased CC:IT ratio of 0.25 (intercaudate distance to inner table width ratio) (Figure 6). Volume loss of the putamen and globus pallidus was noted bilaterally. An enlargement of the bilateral frontal horns of the lateral ventricles, giving a box-like configuration, is noted. There is evidence of T2/FLAIR hyperintensities (Figures 3 and 4) seen in the involved region with mild grade II mineralisation seen in globi pallidi, extending to bilateral posterolateral putamen and also in the substantia nigra. Mild prominence of sulci and ventricles with grey matter volume loss is suggestive of mild cerebral volume loss. MRS shows choline peak and mild NAA (N-acetyl aspartate) loss in the basal ganglia (Figures 7a, 7b, 7c, and 7d).

- Cognitive decline with memory loss, disorientation, and functional dependence developing after onset of movement disorder.  
- MRI shows bilateral caudate atrophy with decreased frontal horn to intercaudate ratio and increased intercaudate to inner table ratio.  
- Volume loss of putamen and globus pallidus bilaterally.  
- Enlargement of bilateral frontal horns of lateral ventricles, producing a box-like dilatation (indicative of caudate atrophy).  
- T2/FLAIR hyperintensities in involved basal ganglia regions with mild grade II mineralization in globus pallidis extending to bilateral posterolateral putamen and substantia nigra.  
- Mild cerebral volume loss with grey matter atrophy.  
- MRS shows choline peak and mild NAA loss in basal ganglia (suggesting gliosis/metabolic stress).  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Chorea-acanthocytosis:  
Pros: Presence of choreiform movements and cognitive decline fits. Caudate and basal ganglia involvement on MRI is compatible.  
Cons: Chorea-acanthocytosis usually presents later in life (often third to fifth decade), with prominent hematologic abnormalities such as acanthocytes in peripheral blood; no mention of acanthocytes or blood film findings here. Also, T2 hyperintensities and mineralization in globus pallidus are not classic for chorea-acanthocytosis.  

2) Huntington’s disease:  
Pros: Classic presentation with choreiform movements, progressive cognitive decline, and 33-year-old patient fits typical onset. Bilateral caudate atrophy with box-like frontal horn dilation is a hallmark MRI finding for Huntingtonâs disease. Basal ganglia volume loss and signal changes (T2/FLAIR hyperintensity, mineralization) fit advanced disease. MRS findings of choline peak and NAA loss also consistent with neuronal loss and gliosis in Huntingtonâs.  
Cons: Mineralization in globus pallidus is less commonly described but can be seen in advanced disease or chronic changes. Age is typical. No specific info about genetic testing but imaging findings are classic.  

3) Neuroacanthocytosis syndrome (umbrella term including chorea-acanthocytosis and McLeod syndrome):  
Pros: Similar clinical features to chorea-acanthocytosis. Basal ganglia involvement expected.  
Cons: Again, no mention of acanthocytes or blood abnormalities; MRI features of caudate atrophy and box-like ventricles are more characteristic of Huntingtonâs; neuroacanthocytosis imaging generally shows less pronounced caudate atrophy.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Huntingtonâs disease (most consistent with classic clinical presentation and hallmark imaging findings of bilateral caudate atrophy with box-like frontal horn dilatation and basal ganglia volume loss).  
2) Chorea-acanthocytosis (clinical symptoms present, but imaging less characteristic and no hematologic confirmation).  
3) Neuroacanthocytosis syndrome (umbrella term, less specific, no supporting lab data).  
</analysis>  
<final> Huntington’s disease </final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: 8-year-old female with swelling in the dorsal aspect of the tongue on the left side, present since birth, with slight enlargement in the past 6 months. No history of fever, breathing difficulties, or bleeding tendency. On physical examination, it showed a bluish swelling in the anterior tongue measuring 1.5 x 1 cm, and it was a soft, non-tender mass on palpation (Figure 1).
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound of the dorsal aspect of the tongue showed a well-circumscribed hypoechoic lesion with smooth margins in the anterior aspect of the tongue on the left side. No evidence of calcifications (phleboliths) or cystic areas. No surrounding induration was seen. Colour Doppler studies showed mild internal vascularity (Figure 2).
 
 MRI showed a well-defined T2 intensely hyperintense (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c), and iso to slightly hyperintense signal in T1-weighted images (Figure 5) in the anterior aspect of the tongue on the left side. No blooming artefacts on the gradient echo sequences (GRE) images (Figure 4). No evidence of true diffusion restriction. Homogeneous contrast enhancement on post-contrast T1 images was seen (Figure 6). Delayed post-contrast images are not available due to patient motion artefacts.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 6-year-old child with a diagnosis of Gorlin syndrome, so far manifested by medulloblastoma resected two years ago, and disease-free in subsequent follow-ups until now, presented with insidious hip pain. Given her medical history, a pelvic CT was performed.
 Imaging Findings: Brain MRI, performed 2 years previously, at the time of medulloblastoma diagnosis, revealed a mottled intraparenchymal mass in the posterior fossa with heterogeneous enhancement and hydrocephalus (Figures 1a and 1b).
 
 Actual pelvic CT showed extensive osteolytic involvement with a soft tissue component affecting all included bony structures (vertebral bodies, pelvic girdle, and femurs), with severe destruction of the pelvis and both femoral heads (Figures 2a and 2b). Given the presence of lytic metastases, a subsequent whole-body CT with intravenous contrast in the portal phase was performed, revealing retroperitoneal adenopathic conglomerates (Figure 3) and a single hepatic metastasis (Figure 4).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 56-year-old woman presented with a painless right breast lump, present for three months. She also reported easy fatigability, ptosis, and exertional dyspnoea for one year. She was diagnosed with myasthenia gravis, and was started on immunosuppressants five months ago. There was no history of tuberculosis (TB) or contact with TB patients.
 Imaging Findings: High-resolution CT of the thorax showed a well-defined soft tissue density in the anterior superior mediastinum. CT-guided biopsy revealed polygonal epithelial cells with interspersed lymphocytes, suggestive of thymoma type B1. Only non-contrast-enhanced CT of the thorax was performed (Figures 1a, 1b, and 1c) due to contraindication to iodinated contrast in myasthenia gravis. An elliptical pleural-based hyperdense lesion with peripheral calcification and central fluid density was noted along the lateral right hemithorax, suggestive of empyema. A lobulated soft tissue lesion was seen in the lower inner quadrant of the right breast, extending into the underlying pectoral muscle. Ultrasound correlation was advised.
 
 Ultrasound of the right breast (Figures 2a, 2b, and 3) revealed an irregular, heterogeneously hypoechoic lesion with posterior enhancement, oriented parallel to the parenchyma from 3 to 5 o’clock position, 2 cm from the nipple–areola complex. No internal vascularity or calcification was seen. The lesion extended into the pectoralis muscle and appeared soft on elastography. It was categorised as BIRADS 4A.
 
 Fine-needle aspiration cytology was advised (Figures 4a and 4b).

She was diagnosed with myasthenia gravis, and was started on immunosuppressants five months ago. There was no history of tuberculosis (TB) or contact with TB patients.
 Imaging Findings: High-resolution CT of the thorax showed a well-defined soft tissue density in the anterior superior mediastinum. CT-guided biopsy revealed polygonal epithelial cells with interspersed lymphocytes, suggestive of thymoma type B1. Only non-contrast-enhanced CT of the thorax was performed due to contraindication to iodinated contrast in myasthenia gravis. An elliptical pleural-based hyperdense lesion with peripheral calcification and central fluid density was noted along the lateral right hemithorax, suggestive of empyema. A lobulated soft tissue lesion was seen in the lower inner quadrant of the right breast, extending into the underlying pectoral muscle. Ultrasound correlation was advised. Ultrasound of the right breast revealed an irregular, heterogeneously hypoechoic lesion with posterior enhancement, oriented parallel to the parenchyma from 3 to 5 o’clock position, 2 cm from the nipple–areola complex. No internal vascularity or calcification was seen. The lesion extended into the pectoralis muscle and appeared soft on elastography. It was categorised as BIRADS 4A. Fine-needle aspiration cytology was advised.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Breast carcinoma, Plasma cell mastitis, Idiopathic granulomatous mastitis, Mammary tuberculosis, Actinomycosis

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 56-year-old woman with painless right breast lump and myasthenia gravis (associated thymoma type B1 on CT-guided biopsy).  
- Right lower inner quadrant breast lesion irregular, heterogeneously hypoechoic, extending into pectoralis muscle, no internal vascularity or calcification, soft on elastography.  
- Lesion rated BIRADS 4A (moderate suspicion, biopsy recommended).  
- No history of TB or contact with TB patients.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Breast carcinoma  
   - Pros: Irregular, hypoechoic lesion extending into pectoralis muscle is typical for invasive carcinoma; BIRADS 4A suggests suspicious but not definitive; myasthenia gravis can be associated with malignancies.  
   - Cons: No internal vascularity or calcifications, but some cancers can lack these. Elastography softness is atypical (usually harder on stiffness); however, not definitive.  

2) Plasma cell mastitis  
   - Pros: Can present as painless mass, heterogeneous signal.  
   - Cons: Rare; typically associated with lactation or oral contraceptive use; no mention of such history. Elastography typically shows fibrosis/hardness rather than softness. No typical imaging signs like ductal dilation or periductal inflammation.  

3) Idiopathic granulomatous mastitis  
   - Pros: Mimics carcinoma clinically and radiologically; usually lobulated mass; can extend into surrounding tissue.  
   - Cons: Usually younger women, often lactating or postpartum; no mention of such context; elastography may show firmness due to granulomatous tissue.  

4) Mammary tuberculosis  
   - Pros: No TB history, but myasthenia and immunosuppression increase risk; lesion is irregular with central necrosis (fluid density), mimics abscess or caseating granuloma; empyema present in ipsilateral thorax (could be related).  
   - Cons: TB breast involvement is rare; no history of TB; lesion is soft on elastography, which can be consistent with abscess/necrotic tumor rather than solid granulomatous tissue.  

5) Actinomycosis  
   - Pros: Can cause chronic granulomatous infection mimicking neoplasm; can extend into muscle; may show abscess and sinus tracts; immunosuppression is a risk factor.  
   - Cons: Typically causes multiple sinuses, draining fistulae; no mention of such clinical signs; more commonly occurs in cervicofacial region; breast involvement rare.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1) Breast carcinoma - strongly fits imaging criteria (irregular, infiltrative, suspicious lesion), BIRADS 4A, soft tissue invasion, even if no vascularity, no calcification.  
2) Mammary tuberculosis - possible with empyema and immunosuppression but no TB history; TB of breast is rare.  
3) Idiopathic granulomatous mastitis - less likely due to age and no lactating history.  
4) Actinomycosis - rare in breast, no typical draining sinuses mentioned.  
5) Plasma cell mastitis - least likely given clinical context and imaging.  

Therefore, the best diagnosis based on imaging and clinical history is breast carcinoma.  
</analysis>  
<final>Breast carcinoma</final>
<|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 18-year-old female who recently migrated with her family due to war presented with longstanding, progressive motor impairment of unclear origin. She was referred by an outpatient neurologist after multiple sclerosis had been excluded. No previous medical records were available from her country of origin.
 
 According to her mother, she developed normally in her early infancy, walking independently. Around 17–18 months, shortly after vaccination (possibly MMR), she developed dragging of one leg, recurrent fevers, and loss of motor milestones. A verbal diagnosis of post-viral encephalitis was given at the time. Her condition fluctuated, but cognition remained intact. She developed progressive gait decline during her childhood. Wheelchair use began at age 15, and she is now fully dependent for transfers, though she eats and writes independently.
 
 Clinical and neurological examination revealed esophoria, mild tongue deviation, weak facial muscles, hypotrophic upper extremities with reduced fine motor control, joint hypermobility, increased tone and reflexes with bilateral positive Trömner signs, and severe leg-dominant spastic tetraparesis.
 
 Given the unclear clinical presentation, brain and spinal MRI were performed.
 Imaging Findings: Brain MRI revealed symmetrical T2 (Figures 1) and FLAIR (Figures 2) hyperintensities in the supratentorial white matter (excluding U-fibres), splenium of the corpus callosum, posterior limb of the internal capsule, pons along trigeminal pathways, inferior cerebellar peduncles, and pyramidal tracts of the medulla oblongata. DWI (Figures 3) showed restricted diffusion with low ADC (Figures 4). No contrast enhancement was observed (Figures 5). MR spectroscopy (Figures 6) revealed a lactate peak at the level of the abnormal white matter. Spinal MRI (Figures 7 and 8) displayed longitudinal signal abnormalities predominantly involving the dorsal columns and lateral corticospinal tracts throughout the entire cord. Findings suggested a metabolic leukodystrophy. Diagnosis was confirmed by genetic testing.

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>
A) Key findings:
- Young female with progressive motor impairment since early childhood.
- Symmetrical T2 and FLAIR hyperintensities in supratentorial white matter (excluding U-fibers), splenium of corpus callosum, posterior limb of internal capsule, pons along trigeminal pathways, inferior cerebellar peduncles, and pyramidal tracts of medulla.
- Restricted diffusion on DWI/ADC with no contrast enhancement.
- MR spectroscopy shows a lactate peak at sites of abnormal white matter.
- Spinal MRI reveals longitudinal signal abnormalities involving dorsal columns and lateral corticospinal tracts throughout spinal cord.
- Metabolic leukodystrophy suspected; confirmed by genetic testing.

B) Candidate pass/fail:
1) Hypomyelination with brainstem and spinal cord involvement (HBSL):
   - Pros: Involves brainstem and spinal cord, genetic leukodystrophy.
   - Cons: Typically shows hypomyelination pattern not restricted diffusion or lactate peaks; motor symptoms less severe progression with later onset; usually no lactate peak on spectroscopy.
2) Leukoencephalopathy with brainstem and spinal cord involvement and lactate elevation (LBSL):
   - Pros: Pathognomonic symmetrical white matter involvement including corticospinal tracts, brainstem, spinal cord; lactate peak on MR spectroscopy; restricted diffusion consistent with active disease; clinical progressive motor impairment.
   - Cons: None significant; fits clinical and imaging findings perfectly.
3) Krabbe disease:
   - Pros: Leukodystrophy with progressive motor impairment.
   - Cons: Typically infantile onset with frontal white matter involvement and involvement of U-fibers; no characteristic lactate peak; clinical course more acute in infancy.
4) X-linked adrenoleukodystrophy:
   - Pros: Leukodystrophy often involving brainstem and corticospinal tracts.
   - Cons: Usually younger male patients; involves parieto-occipital white matter; presence of adrenal insufficiency; lactate peak not typical; genetic pattern inconsistent with female sex.
5) Metachromatic leukodystrophy:
   - Pros: Progressive demyelination affecting white matter.
   - Cons: Involves parieto-occipital white matter and periventricular areas; no lactate peak; spinal cord involvement not predominant; clinical course and imaging do not match.
6) Alexander disease:
   - Pros: Leukodystrophy with frontal predominance and abnormal brainstem.
   - Cons: Typically shows contrast enhancement; involves frontal lobes and periventricular regions; no spinal cord involvement; lactate peak not a feature.

C) Ranking:
1) Leukoencephalopathy with brainstem and spinal cord involvement and lactate elevation (LBSL) - fits perfectly with symmetrical white matter involvement, brainstem and spinal cord lesions, lactate peak, clinical presentation.
2) Hypomyelination with brainstem and spinal cord involvement (HBSL) - similar but lacks lactate peak and restricted diffusion features.
3) Krabbe disease - less likely due to age and imaging pattern.
4) Metachromatic leukodystrophy
5) Alexander disease
6) X-linked adrenoleukodystrophy

<final>Leukoencephalopathy with brainstem and spinal cord involvement and lactate elevation (LBSL)</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 21-year-old Caucasian man, an amateur drummer, presents with a two-year history of progressive weakness of the left distal hand. There is no associated pain or sensory loss. Past medical and family history are unremarkable. There is no history of preceding trauma.
 Imaging Findings: On MRI performed with the neck in the neutral position, T2 and T1-weighted sagittal images (Figures 1 and 2, respectively) reveal mild spinal cord atrophy at the C5–C7 level. On T2 axial images (Figure 3), atrophy of the left anterior horn is clearly visible.
 
 In the flexed position (Figure 4), there is an anterior shift of the posterior wall of the cervical dural sac at the C3–Th3 level. A crescent-shaped epidural venous plexus appears in the posterior epidural space, showing high signal intensity on both T1 and T2-weighted sequences and is accompanied by flow voids.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 21-year-old Caucasian man, an amateur drummer, presents with a two-year history of progressive weakness of the left distal hand. There is no associated pain or sensory loss. Past medical and family history are unremarkable. There is no history of preceding trauma.
 Imaging Findings: On MRI performed with the neck in the neutral position, T2 and T1-weighted sagittal images reveal mild spinal cord atrophy at the C5–C7 level. On T2 axial images, atrophy of the left anterior horn is clearly visible. In the flexed position, there is an anterior shift of the posterior wall of the cervical dural sac at the C3–Th3 level. A crescent-shaped epidural venous plexus appears in the posterior epidural space, showing high signal intensity on both T1 and T2-weighted sequences and is accompanied by flow voids.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Young-onset amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, Spinal muscular atrophy, Anterior horn syndrome, Hirayama disease
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Young adult male (21 years old)  
- Progressive weakness of left distal hand (motor involvement, no pain or sensory loss)  
- MRI neck in neutral position shows mild spinal cord atrophy at C5-C7, especially T2 and T1 sagittal images.  
- T2 axial images reveal atrophy of left anterior horn cells.  
- Flexed position MRI shows anterior displacement of posterior dural wall at C3-Th3, and crescent-shaped epidural venous plexus with high T1 and T2 signal intensity with flow voids.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Young-onset amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS):  
   - Pros: Progressive motor weakness in a young adult can be seen; upper and lower motor neuron signs are typical in ALS.  
   - Cons: ALS is bilateral and symmetrical usually, also involves both upper and lower motor neurons widely; no mention of upper motor neuron signs or widespread involvement. Imaging findings typically do not show dural displacement or epidural venous plexus changes; ALS does not usually produce cord atrophy limited to anterior horns or positional dural changes.  

2) Spinal muscular atrophy (SMA):  
   - Pros: Atrophy of anterior horn cells is seen in SMA; motor neuron disease.  
   - Cons: SMA is a genetic disorder presenting earlier in life (infancy/childhood) with proximal muscle weakness and hypotonia; this patient is an adult and has only distal hand involvement; no evidence of typical SMA features; also, no positional dural or epidural changes seen in SMA.  

3) Anterior horn syndrome:  
   - Pros: Anterior horn cell involvement is shown by atrophy on MRI; pure motor weakness without sensory or pain. Could be consistent with an anterior horn cell disease.  
   - Cons: This is a clinical syndrome rather than a specific diagnosis; the unique flexion-dependent dural and venous changes are not typical; also, this explanation doesn't account for dynamic cervical changes on flexion.  

4) Hirayama disease:  
   - Pros: Young adult male with progressive distal upper extremity weakness; MRI shows cervical cord atrophy at C5-C7, specifically anterior horn cell involvement; dynamic cervical flexion MRI shows anterior displacement of posterior dural wall, crescent-shaped enhancing epidural venous plexus best seen on flexion MRI; classic feature of Hirayama disease ("flexion myelopathy").  
   - Cons: None significant; findings are textbook for Hirayama disease.  

C) Ranking:
1) Hirayama disease - Fits perfectly with clinical and dynamic MRI findings (young male, distal-upper-extremity motor weakness, anterior horn atrophy, dynamic flexion dural displacement, and epidural venous plexus).
2) Anterior horn syndrome - Compatible with anterior horn atrophy and motor symptoms but lacks dynamic cervical MRI findings.
3) Young-onset ALS - Less likely due to lack of upper motor neuron involvement and no dynamic changes.
4) Spinal muscular atrophy - Unlikely due to age and clinical presentation mismatch.

Therefore, the best diagnosis is Hirayama disease.  
</analysis>  
<final>  
Hirayama disease  
</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 81-year-old man was referred with a four-month history of persistent right upper quadrant pain radiating to the back. Liver function tests were normal, and he denied fever or pruritus. His medical history included pacemaker implantation, malaria, appendicectomy, arterial hypertension, and orthopaedic surgeries. No alcohol abuse or smoking was reported.
 Imaging Findings: The patient first underwent abdominal ultrasound and contrast-enhanced CT scan (Figures 1a and 1b), which revealed consistent findings: gallbladder hydrops with thin walls, moderate dilatation of the intrahepatic right bile ducts, and an ill-defined 1.5 cm soft-tissue lesion at the junction of the cystic duct and the common bile duct.
 
 Gadoxetic acid-enhanced MRI (Figures 2a and 2b) with magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography (MRCP) (Figures 3a and 3b) confirmed tissue infiltration at the junction of the right anterior segmental duct and common bile duct, explaining the dilatation of the right anterior bile ducts.
 
 Endoscopic ultrasound and endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography (ERCP) (Figure 4) were subsequently performed to perform biopsies and to place a plastic biliary stent.
PostDescription:

Gadoxetic acid-enhanced MRI with magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography (MRCP) confirmed tissue infiltration at the junction of the right anterior segmental duct and common bile duct, explaining the dilatation of the right anterior bile ducts. Endoscopic ultrasound and endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography (ERCP) were subsequently performed to perform biopsies and to place a plastic biliary stent.

1) Intraductal papillary neoplasm of the bile duct (IPNB):  
- Pros: Presence of an intraductal lesion causing ductal dilation; typical location near cystic duct/CBD junction; patient age and presentation compatible.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: Case 1
 
 A 28-year-old patient with known homozygous sickle cell disease (HbSS) presented with chronic fatigue, diffuse abdominal pain, and hip joint discomfort exacerbated by walking.
 
 Case 2
 
 A 34-year-old patient with a known history of sickle cell disease underwent CT for left upper quadrant abdominal pain and chronic fatigue.
 Imaging Findings: Case 1
 
 Abdominal CT scan (Figures 1a and 1b) revealed a markedly reduced spleen size, with the parenchyma completely replaced by dense, coarse calcifications. The spleen had irregular but preserved contours. Post-contrast CT images did not show any enhancement within the splenic parenchyma. There was no perisplenic collection or lymphadenopathy.
 
 Bone window CT images through the pelvis revealed bilateral femoral head avascular necrosis (AVN), with subchondral collapse and cortical irregularities (Figures 2a and 2b).
 
 Case 2
 
 Abdominal CT scan (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, and 3d) demonstrated a severely atrophied spleen appearing as a densely calcified structure in the left upper quadrant. The calcifications are confluent and coarse, with no visible soft tissue component. Post-contrast images showed no contrast enhancement, and no other abdominal or bony abnormalities were identified.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 23-year-old female presented with acute pain and swelling in her left ankle following a fall down the stairs. Clinical examination showed severe bilateral pes planus and mild hindfoot varus.
 Imaging Findings: Radiographic evaluation demonstrated flattening and contour irregularity of the middle and lateral thirds of the navicular bone, accompanied by sclerotic bone remodelling (Figure 1a). A step-off deformity was noted at the dorsal aspect between the navicular and cuneiform bones on the lateral view, along with flattening of the talar dome (Figure 1b).
 
 MRI confirmed these findings, revealing deformity and flattening of the central and lateral portions of the navicular bone, with bone marrow oedema predominantly in the central and dorsal regions. In addition, fragmentation of the central navicular bone was observed (Figures 2a, 2b, 3a, 3b, and 4).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 12-year-old boy without significant previous medical history presented in the ultrasound department for evaluation of a palpable lump noticed since the last two weeks in the left breast. The lump was non-tender and showed no changes in the overlying skin. There was no history of medication use. The hormonal profile and routine CBC profile were normal.
 Imaging Findings: Focused high-resolution ultrasound of both breasts and each axilla was performed using a high-frequency linear probe. The ultrasound identified a well-defined, well-encapsulated, hypoechoic lesion with smooth margins (Figure 1). It measured 3.8 x 2 x 3 cm in size and involved the left breast at the 12 o’clock periareolar region. The lesion was oriented parallel to the tissue planes (Figure 3). A single gentle lobulation was seen in this lesion. No significant vascularity was displayed on the colour Doppler study (Figure 2). The right breast was normal, and both axillae were normal.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 4-month-old male child from a tuberculosis (TB) endemic region presented with complaints of poor feeding, loss of weight for a month, and a low-grade, evening fever without any associated rash or chills for 15 days. The patient also had jerky movements of the right upper and lower limbs without any other signs of seizure disorder.
 
 The patient showed positive Kernig’s (pain and resistance to knee extension on flexing the patient’s hip and knee to 90 degrees and then attempting to extend the knee) [1] and Brudziński’s signs (in a patient with supine position, positive Brudziński’s is involuntary flexion of the hips and knees when the patient’s neck is passively flexed) [2].
 Imaging Findings: As the patient showed positive Kernig’s and Brudziński’s signs, meningeal TB was suspected. However, the chest radiograph was normal. On MRI, subtle T2 FLAIR (fluid attenuation inversion recovery) hyperintense minimally enhancing foci were seen in the bilateral cerebral cortex, caudate nucleus, thalami, and right hemipons with diffusion restriction and without blooming on gradient echo (GRE) sequences (Figures 1a and 1b). Subtle areas of leptomeningeal enhancement along the bilateral frontoparietal region were also seen. Mild communicating hydrocephalus was seen.
 
 A follow-up scan was advised after 4 months of antituberculosis treatment (ATT), as the patient had reduced activity, drowsiness, and episodes of seizure. On follow-up MRI, multiple conglomerated T2 hyperintense T1 fat-sat ring-enhancing lesions were seen in bilateral gangliocapsular and periventricular regions with leptomeningeal enhancement and hydrocephalus (Figures 2a and 2b). Mild surrounding cerebral oedema was also noted.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An eight-month-old female child presented with jaundice and abdominal distension. Clinical examination revealed icterus, a distended stomach, and prominent superficial veins. Laboratory reports showed elevated serum glutamic pyruvic transaminase and serum glutamic oxaloacetic transaminase levels with conjugated hyperbilirubinemia.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound (USG) showed grossly coarsened echotexture of liver with multiple tiny communicating and non-communicating cystic lesions (Figures 1a and 1c). A slightly irregular liver outline was also noted (Figure 1c), indicating liver cirrhosis. Figure 1b shows a large intrahepatic cyst near the porta hepatis containing echogenic debris in dependent location, measuring 2.2 x 1.1 cm (indicating Caroli disease). Many sections also showed ill-defined hypoechoic nodules in the liver, indicative of dysplastic nodules (Figure 1d). The gallbladder was distended, showing an elongated gallbladder neck with a thickened wall (2.4 mm) and increased pericholecystic echogenicity (Figure 2a). Mild dilatation of the cystic duct was also noted (Figure 2b). Another section showed both liver and right kidney in profile with increased renal parenchymal echotexture (Figure 3). Colour Doppler assessment showed absent colour flow in cystic structures, suggesting cystic lesions to be biliary dilatations (Figures 4a and 4b). A splenomegaly of 8 cm with a dilated (10 mm) splenic vein was noted (Figure 6).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 11-year-old girl with normal hearing and cognition had severe dysarthria and language delay since birth. Neurological exam showed no motor deficits but significant expressive impairment. Given the persistent speech delay, a brain MRI was requested for further evaluation.
 Imaging Findings: Brain MRI images demonstrated the eye-of-the-tiger sign. There is marked hypointensity of the globus pallidus on T2-weighted and FLAIR sequences secondary to iron deposition, containing minimal dot-like hyperintensities (vacuolisation), associated with comma-shaped high-signal intensity in the antero-medial part of the globus pallidus, likely due to gliosis (Figures 1 and 2). There is also marked hypointensity on both T2*EG and DWI (diffusion-weighted images) sequences due to susceptibility effects (Figures 3 and 4).
PostDescription:

There is also marked hypointensity on both T2*EG and DWI (diffusion-weighted images) sequences due to susceptibility effects.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A nine-year-old boy, previously healthy, presented with fever, cough, dyspnoea, vomiting and diarrhoea. Eighteen days after hospital admission, he had the first spontaneous bilateral pneumothorax, needing drainage, intensive care unit admission, and mechanical ventilation. After 3 months, even with remaining lung lesions and pneumothorax, he could be discharged for outpatient follow-up.
 Imaging Findings: The initial imaging exam was a chest radiograph (X-ray) showing a bilateral diffuse micronodular acinar infiltrate (Figures 1a and 1b). A computed tomography (CT) was performed, which revealed a diffuse micronodular pattern and random distribution throughout the lung parenchyma, suggestive of an inflammatory/infectious process (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c).
 
 The patient experienced several pneumothoraces, all of which were drained. Up to three chest tubes were placed simultaneously (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, 3d, 3e, and 3f). A second contrast-enhanced chest CT revealed confluent cystic lesions, prevailing on the upper lobes and posterior regions (Figures 4a, 4b, 4c, 4d, 4e, and 4f).
 
 A few weeks later, another contrast-enhanced chest CT showed the same findings, but with larger pneumothoraces on both sides, despite the presence of three thoracic drains (Figures 5a, 5b, 5c, 5d, and 5e). Pneumomediastinum was noted.
 
 Before hospital discharge, the patient underwent a new contrast-enhanced chest CT. The lung abnormalities persisted, but the patient was able to tolerate them even after the chest tubes were removed (Figures 6a, 6b, and 6c).
 
 Eight months later, a follow-up chest CT revealed almost complete recovery of the pulmonary abnormalities, with sequelae changes and discreet cystic lesions, and with favourable evolution of the imaging findings over the eleven-month follow-up period (Figures 7a, 7b, and 7c).

   - Pros: Diffuse micronodular pattern and acinar infiltrates can be seen; immunocompromised presentation.
   - Cons: PJP typically causes ground-glass opacities, not cystic lesions or pneumothorax, especially not in healthy children; no evidence of immunodeficiency mentioned; pneumothorax uncommon and cystic changes not typical.

2) Lymphoid interstitial pneumonia (LIP):
   - Pros: Can cause cystic lung changes in children; associated with diffuse infiltrates.
   - Cons: Typically seen in patients with connective tissue disease or immunodeficiency; no such context here; pattern usually peribronchovascular rather than random; may not typically cause recurrent pneumothoraces.

3) Langerhans cell histiocytosis (LCH):
   - Pros: Common in children and adolescents; imaging usually shows cystic lung lesions with nodules; predilection for upper lobes; can cause pneumothoraces; cysts formed by nodules coalescing; may present with diffuse micronodular pattern initially.
   - Cons: Usually smokers in adults; in children, smoking history not required; presentation fits well.

4) Cystic pulmonary tuberculosis:
   - Pros: TB can cause cavity and cyst-like lesions; possible pneumothorax due to cavitary rupture.
   - Cons: Micronodular pattern without cavitation initially; CT findings described as confluent cystic lesions, no mention of cavities with air-fluid levels; no TB risk factors or positivity mentioned; young age without TB risk factors.

5) Lymphangioleiomyomatosis (LAM):
   - Pros: Leads to diffuse cystic lung disease.
   - Cons: Occurs almost exclusively in women of reproductive age; no cystic lung disease in children, especially males; pneumothorax possible but not typical in this demographic.

6) Birt–HoggâDubÃ© syndrome (BHD):
   - Pros: Causes multiple lung cysts, spontaneous pneumothorax.
   - Cons: Usually adults with characteristic cyst distribution; no familial history or other systemic signs; age not typical; also, this syndrome is rare in children.

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:
- Most consistent with Langerhans cell histiocytosis (LCH): diffuse micronodular infiltrate converting to cystic lesions, upper lobe predominance, recurrent pneumothoraces.
- Lymphoid interstitial pneumonia less likely due to lack of connective tissue disease or immunodeficiency context and typical distribution.
- Pneumocystis pneumonia unlikely given cystic changes and pneumothorax in a previously healthy child without immunosuppression.
- Cystic TB less compatible with imaging pattern and clinical context.
- LAM and BHD far less likely given age and gender.

Thus, the best fit is Langerhans cell histiocytosis. </analysis> <final> Langerhans cell histiocytosis </final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 73-year-old female patient underwent follow-up at our institution for 14 years due to a pancreatic head lesion. The lesion was monitored at six-month intervals through ultrasound, computed tomography, or magnetic resonance imaging, revealing sustained growth throughout the surveillance period. However, the patient remained asymptomatic, and laboratory tests were unremarkable.
 Imaging Findings: Cross-sectional imaging of the upper abdomen, namely computed tomography (CT) and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), revealed a multicystic lesion in the pancreatic head, with lobulated margins, enhancing internal septations (Figures 1, 2, and 3) and a central fibrotic scar with stellate calcifications (Figures 1a–1h). On ultrasound, the lesion depicts multiple hypoechoic/anechoic cysts and hyperechoic septations (Figure 4).
 
 Over time, the lesion’s enlargement led to gradual dilatation of both the bile ducts and the main pancreatic duct. In the most recent magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography (MRCP) (Figures 2a–2i), the pancreatic lesion measured approximately 97 x 86 x 126 mm (length x width x anteroposterior axis). The common bile duct had a calibre of 15 mm, and the main pancreatic duct had a maximum calibre of 7 mm. The pancreatic parenchyma was atrophic.

A) Key findings:  
- 73-year-old female with a 14-year follow-up of a pancreatic head lesion showing sustained growth but asymptomatic with unremarkable labs.  
- Imaging shows a multicystic lesion with lobulated margins in the pancreatic head.  
- Presence of enhancing internal septations and a central fibrotic scar with stellate calcifications.  
- On US, multiple hypoechoic/anechoic cysts with hyperechoic septations.  
- Gradual dilatation of both bile ducts (common bile duct up to 15 mm) and main pancreatic duct (up to 7 mm).  
- Lesion size nearly 10 cm in one dimension (97 x 86 x 126 mm).  
- Atrophic pancreatic parenchyma.

These imaging features are classic for a serous cystadenoma, specifically with the characteristic central scar and stellate calcifications. The multicystic appearance with enhancing septa and lobulation further supports this. While other cystic pancreatic neoplasms (like mucinous cystic neoplasm or intraductal papillary mucinous neoplasm) can have cystic components and ductal dilatation, they generally lack the central scar and stellate calcifications typical of serous cystadenomas. Also, mucinous cystic neoplasms more often occur in younger women and commonly involve the body or tail rather than the head, and IPMNs tend to involve the ductal system more diffusely and produce mucin. Solid pseudopapillary neoplasms are solid-cystic but have different demographics and imaging characteristics.

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
- Giant microcystic serous cystadenoma of the pancreas: Pros - central scar with stellate calcifications and multilocular cystic appearance match. Cons - lesion size very large (giant) but central scar typical.  
- Macrocystic serous cystadenoma of the pancreas: Pros - cystic lesion with septations; macrocystic variant can be large and paucicystic, may not show classic central scar or stellate calcifications. Cons - presence of central scar and stellate calcifications point more to classic microcystic type.  
- Mucinous cystic neoplasm of the pancreas: Pros - cystic lesion in pancreas. Cons - lacks central scar and stellate calcifications; usually in the body/tail; patient demographics and imaging don't fit.  
- Intraductal papillary mucinous neoplasm (IPMN) of the pancreas: Pros - duct dilatation can be seen; but lesion predominantly cystic without solid components or enhancing septations; no communication with duct described; no mucin. Cons - no communication with duct, typical features not present.  
- Solid pseudopapillary neoplasm of the pancreas: Pros - occurs in women; cystic and solid components. Cons - no mention of solid components or calcifications typical of this tumor; imaging description does not match.

C) Ranking:  
1) Giant microcystic serous cystadenoma of the pancreas (pathognomonic central scar with stellate calcifications and multicystic lesion)  
2) Macrocystic serous cystadenoma of the pancreas (cystic but fewer septations and usually no central scar)  
3) Intraductal papillary mucinous neoplasm (lack of typical duct communication or mucin production)  
4) Mucinous cystic neoplasm (typically head-body/tail, no central scar or stellate calcifications)  
5) Solid pseudopapillary neoplasm (solid components not described, imaging more cystic and multicystic)  

Considering the classic central scar with stellate calcifications and enhancing septations in a cystic lesion of the pancreatic head, the diagnosis best fitting is Giant microcystic serous cystadenoma of the pancreas.  
</analysis> <final>Giant microcystic serous cystadenoma of the pancreas</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 78-year-old female presented to the surgery outpatient department with complaints of painless swelling, posterior to the left knee, for 20 years, with a recent onset of pain (since 3 months) after getting hit by an animal at the site of swelling. On examination, the swelling was firm and lobulated with no overlying skin changes. There was a history of weight loss and loss of appetite. No other comorbidities. No family history of malignancy.
 Imaging Findings: A plain lateral radiograph of the knee demonstrated a soft tissue lesion seen on the posterior aspect of the knee (Figure 1).
 
 Soft tissue ultrasound (US) demonstrated a large lobulated and irregularly shaped heterogeneously hypoechoic mass lesion with internal vascularity, seen posterior to the left knee (Figure 2 ). A few central hypoechoic necrotic/cystic areas were also seen. The mass was also seen displacing the popliteal vessels superficially.
 
 MRI (with contrast) findings revealed a large T1 heterointense lobulated mass lesion in the popliteal fossa with patchy areas of T1 hyperintensity (Figure 3). It also showed heterointense signal on T2W images (Figure 4). The lesion also showed patchy areas of diffusion restriction on DWI and ADC (Figures 5a and 5b) and heterogeneous enhancement on post-contrast study (Figure 6). It was seen displacing and causing near-complete encasement of the popliteal neurovascular bundle. There was no obvious involvement/infiltration of the overlying skin.

   Pros: Known to arise in tendons/aponeuroses of extremities, predominantly lower extremities, can present as a deep soft tissue mass. It often shows T1 hyperintensity due to melanin content (clear cell sarcoma is melanocytic).  
   Cons: Typically seen in adolescents/young adults (our patient is elderly), and usually not for 20 years without symptoms. Rare in elderly.  

2) Malignant melanoma:  
   Pros: Commonly melanotic lesion, T1 hyperintensity due to melanin, can occur in soft tissues, can cause neurovascular encasement.  
   Cons: Usually primary skin lesion or metastasis; no skin involvement/Infiltration here and no history of melanoma. Rarely presents as a primary deep soft tissue mass. Also, location posterior knee is uncommon.

   Cons: Very rare entity; clinical and imaging features can overlap with clear cell sarcoma but with more atypical presentation.

   Pros: PVNS can involve popliteal fossa and knee joint.  
   Cons: Usually arises from synovium, not a large lobulated mass displacing/neurovascular encasement; malignant transformation rare; no evidence of joint involvement or typical hemosiderin deposition; lesion is extrinsic.  

5) Synovial sarcoma:  
   Pros: Common in young adults, can occur in soft tissues near joints, can be lobulated and necrotic, shows heterogeneous enhancement. Can be T2 hyperintense, sometimes T1 heterogenous, can encase vessels.  
   Cons: Age is older than typical; usually located near but not within the popliteal fossa; melanin-related T1 hyperintensity not typical.  

6) Alveolar soft part sarcoma:  
   Pros: Rare soft tissue sarcoma with T1 hyperintense areas due to intracellular crystals; common in young adults; usually deep.  
   Cons: Typically seen in adolescents/young adults, not elderly; location and imaging features less typical; clinical course less indolent.  

7) Malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour (MPNST):  
   Pros: Can encase nerves and vessels; occurs in deep soft tissue; can be lobulated; often hypervascular with necrosis; can cause pain. Age typical.  
   Cons: Usually associated with neurofibromatosis; no mention of skin changes or nerve sheath origin. No melanin or T1 hyperintensity typical.

- Both can show melanin-related T1 hyperintensity, but the long duration (20 years) also fits indolent nature of low-grade sarcoma with malignant transformation.  

Therefore, the most likely diagnosis is "Clear cell soft tissue sarcoma with melanocytic differentiation."

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 36-year-old male, HIV-1 positive since age 22 with no follow-up for 4 years, presented with progressive dyspnoea, cough, thoracic pain, and fatigue. Initially treated for presumed community-acquired pneumonia with partial symptom improvement, but later experienced worsening respiratory symptoms. Denied fever or night sweats.
 Imaging Findings: Chest X-ray revealed mediastinal widening, likely due to a mass, accompanied by ill-defined lower lung opacities and bilateral pleural effusion, more pronounced on the right (Figure 1).
 
 Thoracic CT demonstrated a large anterior mediastinal mass (10.8 × 5.3 × 7.6 cm), heterogeneously enhancing with cystic areas and displacement of adjacent vascular structures without invasion, likely representing a conglomerate of necrotic hypervascular lymph nodes (Figure 2). Additional mediastinal adenopathies were noted.
 
 Pulmonary findings included bilateral “flame-shaped” peribronchovascular opacities (Figure 3a), patchy ground-glass opacities, and interlobular septal thickening (Figure 3b). Bilateral pleural effusion was also evident. Systemic hypervascular adenopathies involved the axillary, mesenteric, and inguinal regions bilaterally (short diameter up to 1.7 cm) (Figures 4a, 4b, and 4c).
 
 Abdominal imaging revealed cutaneous millimetric nodules in the thoracic and abdominal walls (Figure 5), hepatomegaly (17.9 cm in craniocaudal length), and gastric wall thickening (Figures 6a and 6b).

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:

- Chest X-ray: mediastinal widening due to a mass; bilateral lower lung opacities and bilateral pleural effusions (right > left).

   - Pros: Mediastinal mass, widespread adenopathy, common in HIV patients.  
   - Cons: Typically lymphomas do not present with extensive hypervascular lesions with cystic necrosis on CT, and hypervascular peribronchovascular opacities and cutaneous nodules are less typical. Lymphomatous adenopathies are usually homogeneous and solid.  

2) Atypical pneumonia:  
   - Pros: Symptoms and lung opacities support infection.  
   - Cons: Mediastinal mass and widespread hypervascular adenopathy and cutaneous nodules are not features of pneumonia.  

3) Mycobacterial infection:  
   - Pros: Lung involvement and mediastinal lymphadenopathy possible.  
   - Cons: Hypervascular adenopathies with cystic necrosis and cutaneous nodules less typical; also no fever/night sweats.  

4) Multicentric Castleman disease:  
   - Pros: Presents with hypervascular mediastinal mass and lymphadenopathy in multiple sites; known association with HIV.  
   - Cons: Usually does not cause extensive pulmonary flame-shaped opacities; not typical for cutaneous nodules and gastric wall thickening; systemic involvement often less extensive.  

5) Disseminated AIDS-related Kaposi sarcoma:  
   - Pros: Classic in HIV patients; disseminated hypervascular lesions in lymph nodes, lungs (flame-shaped peribronchovascular opacities), pleural effusions, cutaneous nodules, gastric wall thickening, hepatomegaly. Mediastinal mass with heterogeneous enhancement and cystic areas consistent with necrotic nodal involvement in KS.  
   - Cons: None significant; findings are quite characteristic.  

6) Lymphangitic carcinomatosis:  
   - Pros: Flame-shaped peribronchovascular opacities and interlobular septal thickening are typical.  
   - Cons: Rare in young HIV patients; no known primary malignancy; mediastinal mass and systemic hypervascular adenopathy and skin nodules not typical.  

7) Pulmonary oedema:  
   - Pros: Interlobular septal thickening can be seen.  
   - Cons: Mediastinal mass, hypervascular adenopathy, cutaneous nodules, and lack of typical heart failure signs makes it unlikely.  

8) Sarcoidosis:  
   - Pros: Bilateral lymphadenopathy, lung involvement.  
   - Cons: Typically non-hypervascular lymph nodes; no cystic necrosis; no cutaneous nodules or gastric wall thickening; mediastinal mass highly unusual.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1) Disseminated AIDS-related Kaposi sarcoma (best fit with all findings, classic HIV-associated disseminated KS pattern).  
2) Multicentric Castleman disease (possible but less likely given lung and skin findings).  
3) Lymphoma (possible but lacks characteristic hypervascular and necrotic features).  
4) Mycobacterial infection, Lymphangitic carcinomatosis, Sarcoidosis, Pulmonary oedema, Atypical pneumonia (less likely).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 19-day-old female newborn was brought to our hospital because of left breast tumefaction for 2 days, with localised erythema and irritability. No fluctuance or nipple discharge was noted. No fever was present. The white blood cell count was elevated. The delivery was eutocic (40 weeks and 2 days) and unremarkable.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 30‐year‐old man presented with two months of right knee pain and swelling. He had recovered from COVID‑19 pneumonia three months earlier after receiving systemic steroids. During his stay in the hospital, the patient has received dexamethasone injection 8 mg/day through the intravenous route for 10 days with gradual tapering over the next 2 weeks. No evidence of diabetes mellitus or other systemic comorbidities. Localised tenderness over the patella was noted; there were no systemic symptoms or history of trauma.
 Imaging Findings: Radiographs of the knee in anteroposterior, lateral, and skyline views revealed superficial sclerosis of the patella, along with a subtle erosion at its inferior pole (Figures 1a and 1b). There were no signs of fracture or significant soft tissue injury. Given the clinical context and radiographic findings, infection was suspected, and an MRI was recommended.
 
 On MRI, the patellar inferior pole appeared hypointense on T1 images (Figures 2a and 2b) and hyperintense on proton density (PD) fat-saturated sequences (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c). Diffusion-weighted images (DWI) also indicated restriction (Figures 4a and 4b). The subchondral bone and articular cartilage remained largely intact, although a mild effusion was present. Post-contrast images showed thickening and enhancement of the synovium (Figures 5a and 5b). Osteomyelitis of the patella was considered likely.

7) Neoplasm (osteochondroma) (reject)  

Given that the differential includes fungal osteomyelitis explicitly and the clinical context is immunosuppressed post-steroid use with imaging findings indicative of infection, fungal osteomyelitis is the best fit.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 45-year-old female presented with a longstanding history of primary amenorrhea and cyclical lower abdominal pain since adolescence. The pain occurs every month, typically lasting 3–5 days, and is described as crampy and localised to the lower abdomen and pelvis.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound examination done on a Samsung RS80A ultrasound machine showed a bulky uterine body with hypoechoic endometrial collection (Figure 1). A heterogeneously hypoechoic lesion with internal echogenic debris was noted in the right adnexa, arising from the right ovary (Figure 2). The left ovary appears normal.
 
 Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) was performed using a 1.5 Tesla GE MR machine. T2-weighted sagittal and axial images of the MRI pelvis show the presence of the fundus and body of the uterus, which is oriented in the right lateral position in the pelvic cavity, with the absence of the cervix (Figure 3). However, the vagina is visualised. T1 hyperintense and T2 mildly hypointense endometrial collection is noted within the endometrial cavity (Figures 3 and 4). A well-defined T1 hyperintense and predominantly T2 hypointense (T2 shading sign) cystic lesion with a thin wall is noted in the right adnexa, arising from the right ovary (Figures 5 and 6). Minimal pelvic ascites is noted.

- Pros: Ovarian endometrioma explained.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 54-year-old man known to have hypertension and type 2 diabetes experienced paraesthesia in his left hand and distal part of his left forearm for several months. On examination, multiple well-defined skin nodules were found, globally distributed, along with reduced sensation distally in the left upper limb.
 Imaging Findings: MRI of the brachial plexus with intravenous contrast showed multiple well-marginated lesions with heterogeneous signal in the infraclavicular part of the left brachial plexus, including the trunks and divisions (Figures 1, 2a, 2b, and 3), extending into the axilla and proximal parts of the upper arm, suggestive of neurofibromas.
 
 MRI of the spine and brain showed no abnormalities, including vestibular schwannoma, and the patient was diagnosed with schwannomatosis.
PostDescription:

MRI of the spine and brain showed no abnormalities, including vestibular schwannoma, and the patient was diagnosed with schwannomatosis.

A) Key findings:  
- Patient: 54-year-old man with hypertension and type 2 diabetes.  
- Clinical presentation: Paraesthesia in the left hand and distal left forearm, reduced sensation distally in the left upper limb, multiple well-defined skin nodules globally distributed.  
- Imaging: MRI with contrast shows multiple well-marginated lesions with heterogeneous signal in the infraclavicular part of the left brachial plexus (trunks and divisions), extending to axilla and proximal upper arm.  
- No abnormalities on MRI of spine and brain, including no vestibular schwannoma.  
- Diagnosis given: schwannomatosis.  

B) Candidate pass/fail with pros/cons:  
1) Neurofibromas  
   - Pros: Neurofibromas can appear as well-marginated lesions and present as multiple nodules; neurofibromas commonly involve peripheral nerves including brachial plexus.  
   - Cons: The diagnosis of schwannomatosis is distinct from neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1) where neurofibromas are classic; no stigmata of NF1 mentioned.  

2) Brachial plexus schwannomas  
   - Pros: Schwannomas arise from nerve sheaths and can affect the brachial plexus; schwannomas are well-defined masses.  
   - Cons: The presence of multiple lesions and skin nodules makes multiple schwannomas more likely. The absence of vestibular schwannoma excludes neurofibromatosis type 2 but does not exclude schwannomatosis. However, multiple schwannomas in the brachial plexus plus skin nodules suggest a systemic condition, better explained by schwannomatosis.  

3) Lymphoma  
   - Pros: Lymphoma can present as lymph node or nerve sheath masses and cause neuropathy.  
   - Cons: Multiple well-defined lesions confined to nerve tracts, well-marginated and with nerve distribution, not typical for lymphoma. No systemic signs or typical imaging features of lymphoma (no infiltrative or diffuse pattern, no typical lymph node involvement).  

4) Brachial plexopathy  
   - Pros: The patient has neuropathic symptoms (paraesthesia, sensory loss).  
   - Cons: Imaging findings show distinct nodules rather than diffuse nerve thickening or edema; nerve plexus inflammation or demyelination would not show discrete masses.  

5) Metastases  
   - Pros: Can cause nerve plexus involvement.  
   - Cons: Usually more infiltrative, less well-defined; no primary cancer history noted; pattern and morphology not typical for metastases; also presence of skin nodules further argues against metastases.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1) Neurofibromas (strong contender due to nerve sheath origin and multiple nodules)  
2) Brachial plexus schwannomas (but single or very few lesions typical, not multiple well-defined widespread nodules)  
3) Lymphoma (less likely morphology)  
4) Metastases (unlikely morphology and no primary cancer)  
5) Brachial plexopathy (less likely due to mass-like lesions)  

Given the diagnosis of schwannomatosis and multiple well-defined nodules affecting the brachial plexus and skin nodules, the best fit from the list is "Neurofibromas," as these are typical of schwannomatosis (multiple nerve sheath tumors).  

</analysis>  
<final>  
Neurofibromas  
</final><|return|>

- Patient: 54-year-old man with hypertension and type 2 diabetes.  
- Clinical presentation: Paraesthesia in the left hand and distal left forearm, reduced sensation distally in the left upper limb, multiple well-defined skin nodules globally distributed.  
- Imaging: MRI with contrast shows multiple well-marginated lesions with heterogeneous signal in the infraclavicular part of the left brachial plexus (trunks and divisions), extending to axilla and proximal upper arm.  
- No abnormalities on MRI of spine and brain, including no vestibular schwannoma.  
- Diagnosis given: schwannomatosis.  

B) Candidate pass/fail with pros/cons:  
1) Neurofibromas  
   - Pros: Neurofibromas can appear as well-marginated lesions and present as multiple nodules; neurofibromas commonly involve peripheral nerves including brachial plexus.  
   - Cons: The diagnosis of schwannomatosis is distinct from neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1) where neurofibromas are classic; no stigmata of NF1 mentioned.  

2) Brachial plexus schwannomas  
   - Pros: Schwannomas arise from nerve sheaths and can affect the brachial plexus; schwannomas are well-defined masses.  
   - Cons: The presence of multiple lesions and skin nodules makes multiple schwannomas more likely. The absence of vestibular schwannoma excludes neurofibromatosis type 2 but does not exclude schwannomatosis. However, multiple schwannomas in the brachial plexus plus skin nodules suggest a systemic condition, better explained by schwannomatosis.  

3) Lymphoma  
   - Pros: Lymphoma can present as lymph node or nerve sheath masses and cause neuropathy.  
   - Cons: Multiple well-defined lesions confined to nerve tracts, well-marginated and with nerve distribution, not typical for lymphoma. No systemic signs or typical imaging features of lymphoma (no infiltrative or diffuse pattern, no typical lymph node involvement).  

4) Brachial plexopathy  
   - Pros: The patient has neuropathic symptoms (paraesthesia, sensory loss).  
   - Cons: Imaging findings show distinct nodules rather than diffuse nerve thickening or edema; nerve plexus inflammation or demyelination would not show discrete masses.  

5) Metastases  
   - Pros: Can cause nerve plexus involvement.

5) Brachial plexopathy (less likely due to mass-like lesions)

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 60-year-old female with a known history of chronic kidney disease, on monthly haemodialysis and type 2 diabetes mellitus, presented with confusion and lethargy for the past 2 days. There was no history of trauma, fever, or seizures. On examination, the patient was disoriented but exhibited no focal neurological deficits.
 Imaging Findings: Non-contrast CT (Figure 1) shows bilateral symmetric hypodensity in the lentiform nuclei, with surrounding more hypodense internal and external capsules forming the “lentiform fork sign”. There is a sub-centimetric-sized subcortical calcification in the right parietal lobe.
 
 MRI brain T2-weighted (Figure 2) shows bilateral symmetric hyperintensity in the thalamus, lentiform nuclei, particularly in the putamen and globus pallidus, with marked symmetrical hyperintensity of the internal, external capsules and internal and external medullary laminae.
 
 MRI FLAIR axial section (Figure 3) through the basal ganglia shows symmetric hyperintensity in the external capsule, internal capsule and internal and external medullary laminae on both sides. There is also hyperintensity in bilateral thalamus, putamen and globus pallidi.

A) Key findings:  
- Patient: 60-year-old female with chronic kidney disease on dialysis and diabetes, presenting with confusion and lethargy.

1) Uremic encephalopathy  
   Pros:  
   - Patient is on dialysis, chronic kidney disease; confusion and lethargy are common.  
   - Imaging findings of bilateral lentiform fork sign (lentiform nuclei and surrounding capsules) is classic for metabolic encephalopathy associated with renal failure.  
   - Symmetric involvement of basal ganglia and white matter consistent with metabolic toxicity.  
   Cons:  
   - None significant; classic imaging correlates.

2) Hypoxic-ischaemic encephalopathy  
   Pros:  
   - Bilateral involvement of basal ganglia and thalami can be seen in hypoxic brain injury.  
   - Symmetric lesions can be seen in severe hypoxia.  
   Cons:  
   - No history of hypoxic event, trauma, or seizures.  
   - Lentiform fork sign is not typical for hypoxic injury; usually involves cortical gray matter, watershed zones.  
   - Clinical presentation not consistent with hypoxia.

3) Hypoglycaemic encephalopathy  
   Pros:  
   - Can cause bilateral basal ganglia and thalamic involvement.  
   Cons:  
   - No history of hypoglycemia given.  
   - Lentiform fork sign is not typical of hypoglycaemia.  
   - Clinical scenario and risk factors point more toward renal metabolic toxicity.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Uremic encephalopathy - best fits clinical context and imaging âlentiform fork signâ is pathognomonic.  
2) Hypoglycaemic encephalopathy - possible but less likely due to no clinical context.  
3) Hypoxic-ischaemic encephalopathy - least likely given no hypoxic episode and typical imaging findings differ.  
</analysis><final>Uremic encephalopathy</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 59-year-old man who had undergone a bilateral vasectomy 20 years earlier underwent a scrotal ultrasound due to mild right testicular discomfort. There was no history of trauma or associated symptoms, and no relevant physical examination or laboratory findings. He had no history of travel outside Europe.
 Imaging Findings: An ultrasound scan using a high-frequency linear transducer (Figures 1a, 1b, and 2) revealed multiple tiny hyperechoic foci within the left epididymal body, measuring less than 1 mm and exhibiting continuous oscillatory movement. The right epididymis had a homogeneous echotexture and normal, symmetric vascularisation using Doppler ultrasound. No abnormal findings were observed in the remaining scrotal ultrasound, including the right testicle and epididymis.
PostDescription:

No abnormal findings were observed in the remaining scrotal ultrasound, including the right testicle and epididymis.
